CalifOrnia
SUPERSEDED
Fair P01itical
Practices Cornmission
IN PART
lも
'32
」uly ■6′  ■99■
Honorable Leroy F. Greene
senator, sixth senate District
state capitol, Room 3082
Sacranento, CA 9 5814
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. I -91-2 8 2
Dear Senator Greene:
You have requested advice concerning the new provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Actu) 1 which linit 
the receipt of
gifts and honoraria by public officials. 
The comrnission is
considering a regulation dealing with this issue. but since that
regutation has not yet been adopted, we are declining to provide
fornal advice on this issue. Pursuant to Regulation
18329(b) (8) (E), the Commission may decline to provide formal
advice where the request presents issues requiring a policy
interpretation best made by adoption of a regulation pursuant to
Government code section 83112. we-have, however, provided this
infornal response to your request.z
Please be alrare that since the issues raised in this letter
present significant policy questions which will be deternined by
the commission in the future in the form of a regulation. In the
interin, lre have provided a conservative and cautious
interpretation of the Act.
1^ covernment code Sections 8l,ooo-910L5. AII statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations Section
18OOO, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regulations.
provide the requestor with the
or formal written advice. (Section
2  .nfOrmal assiStance does not
immunity proVided by an opinion
83■■4′  RegulatiOn 
■8329(c)(3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO a P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95804´0807 0 (916)322‐ 5660


Our Fi■e No. 
エー9■-282
Page 2
OUESTION
May you accept pay[ent in compensation for the sale of
articles and children's stories to uagazines and periodicals,
$/here you have been an author fot 25 years but have not requested
or received compensation for your writing?
CONCLUSTON
Pending further decision of the Couuission, the safest course
of action would be to not accept pa)rnent for your rriting 
unless
it is in connection with the practice of a bona fide business,
trade, or profess ion.
FACTS
You are a Senator in the California Legislature. over the
past 25 years you have also been an active writer, having
published approxirnately 1,3o0 nelrspaper columns which are
syndicated by the Capitol Nelrs Service. You have never before
requested or received pa)ment for your writing.
You are now seeking to expand your writing efforts. 
You wish
to subnit articles to nagazines, such as the Californj.a Journal,
for conpensation. You stated the california Journal wiII be
publishing one of your articles in the June 1991 issue. In
addition, you lJish to narket a series of children's fairy tales
which you initially 
rrrote for your daughter. You have become
concerned that accepting conpensation for your rrritten lrork nay
violate the new honoraria prohibition of the Act.
ANALYSIS
Effective January 1, 1991, the Legislature enacted a series
of statutes that ban receipt of honoraria by Public officials.
Section 89502 provides in a pertinent part:
(a) No elected state officer may accePt an
honorar iurn.
(b) rtHonorariumrr means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any pa)rment nade in consideration
for any speech given, article Dublished, or
attendance at any publ.ic or private conference,
convention, neeting, social event, Dea1, or like
gathering.


Our File NO. I-9■
-282
Page 3
/ct 
rrHonorariumri does not include earned
income for personal services which are customarilv
provided in connection with the individual's
business or practice of a bona fide business,
trade, or profession, such as teaching, practicing
Iaw, uedicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, unless the sole or
predominant activity of the business, trade, or
profession is making speeches. The coumission
shaIl. adopt regulations to inplenent this
subdivision.
Section 89502.
Your questions concern the tern "article published'r as used
in Section 89502 of the Act. An Itarticle publishedtr lrould appear
to include manuscripts published in periodicals, journals,
newspapers, newsletters, magazines, panphlets, or any other
similai publications.3 According to your facts, the publication
of your manuscripts lrouId fall within the scope of itarticle
pub-Iished'r as used in Section 89502. Thus, a payment received for
Lhe works you describe would be an trhonorariun[ Pursuant to the
statutory definition in the Act.
However, section 89502 Provides that the term "honorariuD"
does not include earned income for personaL services which are
custornarily provided in connection reith the official's 
bona fide
business or Lrade. what constitutes a rrbona f iderr business is not
specifically defined in the statute.
currently staff is advising that a business would be treated
as irbona f idett if the owner of the business rnaintained books and
records of accounting for the business and tax records filed in
connection with the business for the two calendar years preceding
the payrnent in question. clearly this does not apPly to your
situition since according to your facts you never before treated
your writing as a business.
For a new business, however, the Commission is advising that
the business still 
nay be "bona f iderr if the followj.ng applys
there are tax records filed in connection with the business for
one year; there exist bank accounts or lines of credit in the nane
of t[le business; equiprnent or supplies were purchased, leased, or
rented for the business; you have paid rent, wages, business or
professional insurance, oi other sinilar palrments associated with
3  Proposed Regulation 
■893■.2 is
Please note that the te■
11: ::artiCle
construed.
enclosed for your infornration.
publishedt' rnay be broadlY


Our File No. I-9■
-282
Page 4
the operation of a businessr' you have made efforts to market the
your producti or you have a special expertise in connection with
the service provided. While existence of aII of the factors is
not required, the existence of at least half would indicate that
the buiiness is bona fide.'
Ho$/ever, the commission has not yet determined if the
Legislature intended for the exception to include a business which
is an article-writing 
business. It may be argued that recognizing
an article-writing 
busj.ness as bona fide, and consequently, within
the exception to the honorarium ban, could effectively result in
the exception swallowing up the rule. 
This would appear to
contradict the general rules of statutory construction which
provide that exceptions should be narrowly construed. (Estate of
Baneriee (19?8) 2l ca1.3d 527,54o. ) Here, the exception to
honoraria relates to personal services provided "in connection
withtr the practice of a rrbona fide" business. (Section 89502.)
It could be argued that it r.rould be contradictory to the general
prohibition to construe the statute to merely prohibit pa)rment for
articles unless the official 
characterizes his writing as an
article writing business.
It nay also be argued that section 89502 requires only that
there be a separate underlying business that is not itself article
writing. 
If the underlying business is present, the exception
would apply to articles published in connection uith that
business.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this natter, please feel free
to contact De at (916) 322-59oL.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral counsel

business, trade, or professiontr in the future.
